courses.prerequisites: A pass in any of MAM1004F/S or MAM1005H or MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1020F/S or MAM1010F/S or MAM1110F/H. Note: STA1000F and STA1000S are identical courses offered in first and second semesters. Owing to the mathematics prerequisites, first-year students can only register for STA1000F/S on completion of the mathematics prerequisite. One lecture per week, one workshop per week and one tutorial per week. A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA1000F/S/P/L, STA1007S, STA1006S, STA1008F/S.
courses.course_outline: This is an introductory statistics course aimed at exposing students to principles and tools to support appropriate quantitative analysis. The aim is to produce students with a functional sense of statistics. Topics covered include: exploratory data analysis and summary statistics; probability theory; random variables; probability mass and density functions; Binomial, Poisson, Exponential, Normal and Uniform distributions; sampling distributions; confidence intervals; introduction to hypothesis testing (including tests on means, tabular data and bivariate data); determining sample sizes; simple linear regression and measures of correlation. The course is offered predominantly, but not exclusively, to Commerce students. Students are assessed on their knowledge of the topics covered and their ability to perform simple and appropriate statistical analyses using spreadsheet functions.
courses.lecture_times: One lecture per week, one workshop per week, one tutorial per week.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 2-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in any of MAM1004F/S or MAM1005H or MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1020F/S or MAM1010F/S or MAM1110F/H. Note: STA1000F and STA1000S are identical courses offered in first and second semesters. One lecture per week, one workshop per week, and one tutorial per week. A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA1000F/S/P/L, STA1007S, STA1100S, STA1006S, STA1106H, STA1008F/S.
courses.course_outline: This is an introductory statistics course aimed at exposing students to principles and tools to support appropriate quantitative analysis. The aim is to produce students with a functional sense of statistics. Topics covered include: exploratory data analysis and summary statistics; probability theory; random variables; probability mass and density functions; Binomial, Poisson, Exponential, Normal and Uniform distributions; sampling distributions; confidence intervals; introduction to hypothesis testing (including tests on means, tabular data and bivariate data); determining sample sizes; simple linear regression and measures of correlation. The course is offered predominantly, but not exclusively, to Commerce students. Students are assessed on their knowledge of the topics covered and their ability to perform simple and appropriate statistical analyses using spreadsheet functions.
courses.lecture_times: One lecture per week, one workshop per week, one tutorial per week.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 2-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: Students should have obtained a DP for either STA1000F/S.
courses.course_outline: This is an introductory statistics course aimed at exposing students to principles and tools to support appropriate quantitative analysis. The aim is to produce students with a functional sense of statistics. Topics covered include: exploratory data analysis and summary statistics; probability theory; random variables; probability mass and density functions; Binomial, Poisson, Exponential, Normal and Uniform distributions; sampling distributions; confidence intervals; introduction to hypothesis testing (including tests on means, tabular data and bivariate data); determining sample sizes; simple linear regression and measures of correlation. Students are assessed on their knowledge of the topics covered and their ability to perform simple and appropriate statistical analyses using spreadsheet functions.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of tests and completion of assignments and/or exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 2-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: At least 60% in MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1005H or MAM1020F/S or at least 70% in MAM1010F/S or MAM1004F and concurrent registration for MAM1032S or MAM1034S, or MAM1006H or MAM1012F/S or MAM1021S. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA1000F/S/P/L, STA1007S, STA1006S, STA1008F/S. This is the key entry-point into a Mathematical Statistics major and is compulsory for students intending to major in Mathematical Statistics.
courses.course_outline: This is an introduction to statistics covering foundation knowledge in statistical theory. Topics covered include: Types of data variables; Exploratory data analysis; Grouping and graphing of data; Set theory and counting rules; Probability (conditional probabilities, independence, Bayes theorem); Random variables and values, probability mass and density functions, cumulative distribution functions; Population models and parameters (binomial, Poisson, geometric, negative binomial, hypergeometric, Uniform, Exponential, Gaussian); Expectation; Coefficient of variation; Sampling distributions (t, Chi-square, F); Point and interval estimation; Sample size estimation; Hypothesis testing (Z-test and T-test, F-test, Chi-squared test); Meaning of p-values; Bivariate data (scatterplot, simple linear regression and correlation).
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 4th period.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in any of MAM1004F/S or MAM1005H or MAM1031F or MAM1033F. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA1000F/S/P/L, STA1007S, STA1006S, STA1008F/S. This course is the equivalent of STA1000S in a biological setting.
courses.course_outline: This course aims to provide an introduction to statistics for Science students. Topics covered include: exploratory data analysis and summary statistics; set theory; probability (conditional probabilities, independence, Bayes theorem); random variables; probability mass and density functions; Binomial, Poisson, Exponential, Normal and Uniform distributions; sampling distributions; confidence intervals; hypothesis testing (Z-test and t-test for means and difference between means, Chi-square test for independence and goodness-of-fit); meaning of p-values; determining sample size; simple linear regression and measures of correlation. Practical data analysis is taught using R.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st period.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 3-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in (MAM1000W or MAM1032S or MAM1034S or MAM1012S or MAM1006H) and STA1006S or STA1106H. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for both STA2004F and STA2030S. Intended for students studying Mathematical Statistics or Actuarial Science.
courses.course_outline: STA2004F is a rigorous introduction to the foundation of mathematical statistics, providing students with a deeper understanding of statistical concepts covered in STA1006S. The course is divided into two broad sections: (1) Distribution theory — students learn to derive the distributions of random variables and their transformations, and explore the limiting behaviour of sequences of random variables; and (2) Statistical Inference — covering the estimation of population parameters and hypothesis testing based on a sample of data.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday to Friday, 1st period.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in STA2004F. MAM2011F (Linear Algebra) is strongly recommended. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA2020F/S, STA2007F/H/S, STA2005S.
courses.course_outline: This course gives an introduction to statistical modelling and the theory of linear statistical models. The course has two sections: Regression (the multivariate normal distribution; quadratic forms; the linear model; maximum likelihood; estimates of parameters in the linear model; the Gauss-Markov theorem; variable selection procedures; analysis of residuals, bootstrapping; principal component analysis for dimension reduction and for regression) and Design and analysis of experiments (introduction to basic design principles, basic experimental designs, factorial experiments, analysis of variance, multiple comparisons, power and sample size calculations, random effects and repeated measures, permutation/randomisation tests, nonparametric tests, bootstrapping). Students are introduced to relevant statistical software and practical data analysis through weekly computer practicals.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st period.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.course_title: Study Design & Data Analysis for Scientists
courses.semester: First Semester, Second Semester, or Half Year (F/S/H). Offered in blended learning format.
courses.prerequisites: A pass in (STA1000F/S or STA1006S or STA1007S or STA1106H or STA1100S or STA1008F/S) and (MAM1000W or MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1004F/S or MAM1005H or MAM1010F/S or MAM1020F/S or MAM1110F/H). Note: A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA2020F/S, STA2007F/H/S, STA2005S.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to equip students with practical experience and skills in analysing data using statistical techniques frequently used in the sciences. Skills include designing experiments, choosing appropriate statistical methods for visual display and statistical modelling of data, model checking, interpretation and reporting of statistical results, and understanding of limitations of statistical methods and data. Topics covered include: introduction to statistical notation, linear regression, design and analysis of experiments, generalised linear models. Strong emphasis is placed on practical application using open-source statistical software R. There will be a one-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of the first semester and a one-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of the second semester.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 2-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.course_title: Study Design & Data Analysis for Scientists
courses.semester: Summer Term (P). Offered in blended learning format, dependent on sufficient demand and capacity. Request for offering must come from a UCT course convener.
courses.prerequisites: A pass in STA1007S (preferably) or STA1000F/S or STA1006S or STA1106H or STA1100S or STA1008F/S) and (MAM1000W or MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1004F/S or MAM1005H or MAM1010F/S or MAM1020F/S or MAM1110F/H). Students make use of online learning workshops.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to equip students with practical experience and skills in analysing data and applying statistical techniques relevant to the natural sciences. Skills include designing experiments, choosing appropriate statistical methods for analysing data, visual display and statistical modelling of data, model checking, interpretation and reporting of statistical results. Topics include: introduction to statistical notation, linear regression, design and analysis of experiments, generalised linear models. Strong emphasis on practical application using R. There will be a one-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of each semester.
courses.lecture_times: Blended learning format with online workshops.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 2-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in STA1000F/S/P/L or STA1006S or STA1106H or STA1100S or STA1007S or STA1008F/S and MAM1000W or MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1004F/S or MAM1010F/S or MAM1020F/S or MAM1110F/H or MAM1005H. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA2020F/S, STA2007F/H/S, STA2005S.
courses.course_outline: This course is designed to extend the student's basic knowledge acquired in STA1000F/S/P/L. The emphasis is on applying statistical methods and modelling techniques to data rather than focusing on mathematical rigour. Topics covered include: analysis of variance and experimental design; revision and extension of simple linear regression; multiple regression; logistic regression; model building; time series analysis; and non-parametric statistics. Students will analyse data using R.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures and lab practicals and the completion of class tests, mini practical lab tests, and practical tests as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35% and at least 35% for practical test.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 3-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: A pass in STA1000F/S/P/L or STA1006S or STA1106H or STA1100S or STA1007S or STA1008F/S and MAM1000W or MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1004F/S or MAM1010F/S or MAM1020F/S or MAM1110F/H or MAM1005H. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for more than one of STA2020F/S, STA2007F/H/S, STA2005S.
courses.course_outline: This course is designed to extend the student's basic knowledge acquired in STA1000F/S/P/L. The emphasis is on applying statistical methods and modelling techniques to data rather than focusing on mathematical rigour. Topics covered include: analysis of variance and experimental design; revision and extension of simple linear regression; multiple regression; logistic regression; time series analysis; and non-parametric statistics. Students will analyse data using R.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures and lab practicals and the completion of class tests, mini practical lab tests, and practical tests as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35% and at least 35% for practical test.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 3-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: At least 45% for STA2020F/S or STA2007F/S/H or STA2005S or (DP mark for STA2004F with concurrent registration for STA2020S/STA2007S). Co-requisites: Concurrent registration for MAM1032S or MAM1034S or MAM1012S or MAM1021F/S. Note: A student may not register concurrently for STA2030S and MAM1006H. A student cannot obtain credits for both STA2004F and STA2030S.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to Statistical Theory and Inference. It explores aspects of probability theory particularly relevant to statistics, including random variables, joint probability distributions, expected values and moment generating functions. The course content includes univariate distributions and moments of univariate distributions, moments of bivariate distributions, distributions of sample statistics, and parameter estimation and inference.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 40% and a 3-hour exam counting 60%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: STA2020F/S or STA2007F/S/H, or at least 45% for STA2005S.
courses.course_outline: The aim of the course is to create a practical working familiarity with the analysis of data, focusing on multivariate methods as applied in areas such as marketing, the social sciences and the sciences. Topics covered include item reliability analysis, multidimensional scaling, correspondence analysis, principal component and factor analysis, cluster analysis, discriminant analysis, classification trees and structural equation modelling.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: (STA2030S or STA2004F) and (STA2020F/S / STA2007F/S or 45% STA2005S) and (MAM1032 or MAM1034S or MAM1006S or MAM1012F/S or MAM1021F/S or MAM1112S). Note: A student cannot obtain credits for both STA3030F and STA3041F.
courses.course_outline: This course forms part of the third-year major in Applied Statistics. It consists of 4 modules: (1) Simulation — introduces use of computer simulation and data re-sampling techniques (bootstrap) to investigate one and two sample tests, ANOVA, and properties of distributions; (2) Generalised linear models — introduces the exponential family of distributions and extends linear regression to models for non-normal response variables, including logistic regression; (3) Machine learning — covers a basic introduction to statistical learning paradigms, applications of regression and classification trees, and a primer on feedforward neural networks and backpropagation; (4) Bayesian — introduces decision theory and Bayesian inference. Students will use the R programming language.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: STA2020F/S or STA2005S or STA2007F/S and (MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent). Students must be in their third academic year to take the course. All prerequisite courses must be passed prior to taking STA3036S (not done concurrently).
courses.course_outline: This course forms part of the third-year major in Applied Statistics. It is an introduction to Operational Research (OR) exploring fundamental quantitative techniques with a strong focus on computer-based application. Topics covered include: linear and non-linear programming (finding optimal solutions by characterising problems in terms of objectives, decision variables and constraints); decision making under uncertainty through decision trees, decision rules and scenario planning; Queueing Theory simulation modelling the operation of real-world systems as they evolve over time.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: STA2004F, STA2005S and (MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2012S and MAM2014S) strongly recommended. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for both STA3030F and STA3041F.
courses.course_outline: This course forms part of the third-year major in Mathematical Statistics. It consists of two modules: (1) Stochastic Processes — covering the general theory underlying stochastic processes and their classifications, definitions and applications of discrete Markov chains, branching processes, discrete and continuous time counting processes, and homogeneous and non-homogeneous Poisson processes; (2) Time Series Analysis — covering global and local models of dependence, stationary ARMA processes, unit root processes, and a brief introduction to univariate Volatility models and cointegration.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st period.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: STA2004F, STA2005S and MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2012S and MAM2014S. Concurrent registration for STA3041F required. Note: A student may not register concurrently for STA3045F and CSC2001F. This course is for students studying Actuarial Science or Mathematical Statistics, though not a requirement for a major in Mathematical Statistics.
courses.course_outline: This course begins with a brief introduction to copulas and extreme value theory, together with applications to risk management. The rest of the course gives a theoretical overview of stochastic processes, with models spanning both discrete and continuous time as well as discrete and continuous state-space. Though the emphasis is on theoretical properties of the models, real-world applications are also explored. Topics covered: copulas, extreme value theory, homogenous and non-homogeneous continuous-time Markov chains, random walks, probability theory, martingales, Brownian motion, and diffusion processes.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 2nd period.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.prerequisites: STA2004F, STA2005S and (MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2012S and MAM2014S) strongly recommended. Co-requisites: STA3048S. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for both STA3043S and (STA3047S + STA3048S).
courses.course_outline: Machine learning topics include: a basic introduction to statistical learning paradigms, applications of regression and classification trees, and a primer on feedforward neural networks and backpropagation.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 1st period (see departmental entry). Two practicals per week.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline.
courses.assessment: A computer based exam.

courses.prerequisites: STA2004F, STA2005S and (MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2012S and MAM2014S) strongly recommended. Co-requisites: STA3047S. Note: A student cannot obtain credits for both STA3043S and (STA3047S + STA3048S). This course forms part of the third-year major in Mathematical Statistics.
courses.course_outline: This course consists of three modules: (1) Generalised Linear Models — theory and application of fitting linear models to various types of response variables with different underlying distributions; covers the exponential family of distributions, the GLM formulation, estimation and inference, models for continuous responses with skew distributions, logistic regression, log-linear models and Poisson regression; (2) Machine Learning — elementary concepts and methods in machine learning within the framework of statistical learning; (3) Introduction to Bayesian Analysis — Bayesian paradigm of statistical inference, analysis, and risk theory; covers Bayes' theorem, Bayesian statistical analysis for Bernoulli and normal sampling, empirical Bayes and credibility theory, loss and extreme value distributions, Monte Carlo methods.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 1st period (see departmental entry). Two practicals per week.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance of lectures, tutorials, practicals and tests and completion of assignments and/or class exercises as set out in course outline. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 30% and a 3-hour exam counting 70%. Weights will be adjusted in the case of missed assessments, as detailed in the course outline.

courses.convener: Associate Professor G Distiller and Dr E Pienaar
courses.prerequisites: Minimum requirements: (MAM1031F or MAM1004F/S or MAM1010F/S or MAM1033F) and (MAM1032S or MAM1034S), a first year semester module in Computer Science, plus one of the following: Applied Statistics stream (STA3030F + STA3036S/STA3022F) or Mathematical Statistics Stream (STA3041F, STA3043S). Applicants with ≥65% average for 3rd year Mathematical Statistics courses (first attempt) or ≥70% for Applied Statistics courses (first attempt) can be confident of admission; Applied Statistics students also need ≥60% for each of the two prerequisite courses. Students not meeting threshold levels may be considered case-by-case. Note: STA4007W does not carry NQF credit value; students are concurrently registered for STA4022W (coursework, 120 NQF credits) and STA4023W (research project, 40 NQF credits).
courses.course_outline: This honours programme teaches students the necessary data analytical and computing skills for a career in Data Science. It covers theoretical and applied statistics, supervised and unsupervised learning, statistical computing and operations research. Core modules (81 NQF credits) give a good theoretical basis and statistical computing skills. Elective modules (39 NQF credits) expose students to practical application of quantitative methods in different areas. Supervised project work (40 NQF credits) provides training in research. Elective modules vary from year to year, but typically include Portfolio Theory, Time Series Analysis, Biostatistics, Decision Modelling, and Spatial Statistics.
courses.assessment: Each coursework module comprises tests, assignments and a final examination; coursework weighting varies between 30-50%, final examination up to 70% of module mark. Overall programme mark is a weighted average of all coursework modules and the research project. To qualify, a student must achieve: at least 50% average across all core modules, at least 40% across all elective modules, and a minimum of 50% for both the project and the overall programme mark. Components are combined into a single final mark recorded under STA4007W, with 'PA' entered against coursework and project codes.

courses.prerequisites: Currently doing an honours or master's degree in statistics, or at the discretion of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to provide students with an introduction to the Bayesian method and the acquisition of theory and methods required to apply Bayesian analysis to real world practical problems. Topics included: Bayesian linear regression, variable selection, mixture modelling, Markov chain Monte Carlo methods (theory and practical applications).
courses.assessment: Assignments and Exam.

courses.prerequisites: STA3041F, STA3043S or at the discretion of the Head of Department. STA3045F is strongly recommended.
courses.course_outline: The course introduces the historical development of ideas from economics, gambling and finance with a South African perspective for portfolio control. Topics include: data-wrangling, portfolio optimisation, sequential historic back-testing and simulation in R; static mean-variance theoretical foundations of portfolio choice, asset pricing and market equilibrium; tactical and strategic portfolios; the Generalised Fundamental Law of Asset Management, the Roll Critique and a Bayesian Black-Litterman portfolio choice framework; active management and its complexities; performance attribution; indexation methods. Advanced topics may include cluster-based portfolio choice, risk-parity models and machine-learning extensions.
courses.assessment: Assignments and Exam.

courses.prerequisites: Currently doing an honours or master's degree in statistics, or at the discretion of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to cover advanced concepts in probability and martingale theory, including products and conditioning, analysis, L2 theory of random variables, characteristic functions, convergence and uniform integrability, martingales, square integrable martingales, and local martingales.
courses.assessment: Assignments, Class tests and Exam.

courses.prerequisites: Not specified.
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty. The dissertation will usually consist of a report detailing the conduct and analysis of the results of research performed under the close guidance of a suitably qualified supervisor/s.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.prerequisites: Not specified.
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty. The dissertation will usually consist of a report detailing the conduct and analysis of the results of research performed under the close guidance of a suitably qualified supervisor/s.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.course_title: Statistics and Data Science Minor Dissertation
courses.prerequisites: STA5003W.
courses.course_outline: On successful completion of the coursework component, students will undertake an individual, supervised research project on a suitable topic, the results of which are to be written up as a minor dissertation. The minor dissertation component of the Statistics and Data Science masters specialisation is a research project based on selected research in a methodological or applied field of Statistics or Data Science.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. The coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.semester: Full Year (W). Note: This course may not be offered in 2024.
courses.prerequisites: A good Honours degree including a strong quantitative component (normally at least two years of Mathematics at a tertiary level). Recommendations from at least two referees able to attest to the applicant's academic abilities and suitability.
courses.course_outline: The aim of this one-year course is to provide broad professional training in the principles and tools of operational research (OR), with particular emphasis on application in the context of development and the developing world. The OR in Development programme prepares graduates for a career applying OR to the unique problems of the developing world, such as conflicting objectives in balancing socio-economic development and corrective actions, less reliable infrastructures, and a post-colonial need for community participation in planning. The first academic year is based primarily on coursework, supplemented by group discussions and case studies, and covers the basic techniques of OR and statistics, specific developmental issues, problem structuring and decision analysis.
courses.assessment: Assessed through class assessments and examinations. A pass for this coursework requires an average of 50% over all modules, as well as a minimum of 50% for certain modules designated as core material.

courses.prerequisites: Not specified.
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty. The dissertation will usually consist of a report detailing the conduct and analysis of the results of research performed under the close guidance of a suitably qualified supervisor/s.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.course_title: Study Design & Data Analysis for Scientists Doing Postgraduate Studies
courses.prerequisites: Co-registration for an honours, Master's or PhD degree in a relevant discipline such as Biology, Ocean Science, Medicine, and Engineering that involves a substantial component of quantitative training, as assessed by Head of Statistical Sciences Department. Students must have done a basic (first-year level) course in statistics.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to equip students with practical experience and skills in analysing data using statistical techniques frequently used in the sciences. Skills include designing experiments, choosing appropriate statistical methods for visual display and statistical modelling of data, model checking, interpretation and reporting of statistical results, and understanding of limitations of statistical methods and data. Topics covered include: introduction to statistical notation, linear regression, design and analysis of experiments, generalised linear models. Strong emphasis on practical application using R. There will be a half-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of the first semester and a half-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of the second semester.
courses.assessment: Assignments and quizzes count 20%; one 2-hour examination in June or November counts 80%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.course_title: Study Design & Data Analysis for Scientists Doing Postgraduate Studies
courses.prerequisites: Co-registration for an honours, Master's or PhD degree in a relevant discipline such as Biology, Ocean Science, Medicine, and Engineering that involves a substantial component of quantitative training, as assessed by Head of Statistical Sciences Department. Students must have done a basic (first-year level) course in statistics.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to equip students with practical experience and skills in analysing data using statistical techniques frequently used in the sciences. Skills include designing experiments, choosing appropriate statistical methods for visual display and statistical modelling of data, model checking, interpretation and reporting of statistical results, and understanding of limitations of statistical methods and data. Topics covered include: introduction to statistical notation, linear regression, design and analysis of experiments, generalised linear models. Strong emphasis on practical application using R. There will be a half-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of the first semester and a half-day face-to-face workshop at the beginning of the second semester.
courses.assessment: Assignments and quizzes count 20%; one 2-hour examination in June or November counts 80%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.prerequisites: Successful completion of the coursework component of the Master's course in Biostatistics.
courses.course_outline: This course presents the research component of the Master's course in Biostatistics. The research component is based on a 90 credit dissertation on a topic based on methodological or applied problems from the Health or Biological Sciences. Students may be based in a research unit from where the problem originated. On completion, students will be able to: conduct collaborative research in the health sciences; conduct independent research in statistical methodology for the health sciences; act as statistical consultants for health sciences research; and work with researchers in the biological sciences.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. The coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.prerequisites: Previous exposure to quantitative training that will enable the student to cope with the material in the chosen module plus successful completion of pre-courses deemed necessary, as assessed by Head of the Statistical Sciences Department and the module convener.
courses.course_outline: This module allows students to register for a single module that forms part of the Master's course in Biostatistics. Possible modules include Multivariate Statistics, Longitudinal Data Analysis, Survival Analysis and Design and Analysis of Experiments in the Health Sciences, Advanced Topics in Regression, Simulation and Optimisation, Machine Learning, Bayesian Decision Analysis, Infectious Disease Modelling and Structural Equation Modelling. Students will acquire skills and knowledge of statistical methodology relevant to Health Sciences Research.
courses.assessment: Class assignments 50%; one 3-hour examination counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for both the examination and the class assignments.

courses.prerequisites: Previous exposure to quantitative training that will enable the student to cope with the material in the chosen module plus successful completion of pre-courses deemed necessary, as assessed by Head of the Statistical Sciences Department and the module convener.
courses.course_outline: This module allows students to register for a single module that forms part of the MSc in Biostatistics. Possible modules include Multivariate Statistics, Longitudinal Data Analysis, Survival Analysis and Design and Analysis of Experiments in the Health Sciences, Advanced Topics in Regression, Simulation and Optimisation, Machine Learning, Bayesian Decision Analysis, Infectious Disease Modelling and Structural Equation Modelling. Students will acquire skills and knowledge of statistical methodology relevant to Health Sciences Research.
courses.assessment: Class assignments 50%; one 3-hour examination counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for both the examination and the class assignments.

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This module develops the Bayesian approach to inference and decision making, starting from concepts of subjective probability and subjective expected utility, and moving on to structures of Bayesian modelling for inference, computational solution of such models, and representation of complex learning and decision making processes through Bayesian Networks.
courses.dp_requirements: Completion and submission of the assignment component at a satisfactory grade (40% minimum).
courses.assessment: Assignments 35%. Written examination 65%. A subminimum of 40% in each of the assignments and examination is required.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to the concept of causality, causal diagrams and causal modelling. Topics covered include Counterfactual Theory, Directed Acyclical Graphs, Propensity Scores, Inverse Probability Weighting, Marginal Structural Models, G-estimation, Path Analysis, Confirmatory Factor Analysis, Structural Equation Modelling (SEM), Multiple Group SEM, MIMIC Models, Multilevel SEM, and Latent Growth Curve SEM. The course covers both theory and application using computer software such as R, STATA and LISREL.
courses.assessment: Assignments 40%. Written exam 60%. Sub-minimum of 40% in each of assignment and examination component.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's program in Biostatistics, or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This module covers the Design of Clinical Trials. Concepts of randomisation, replication and blocking are discussed. Students are introduced to the different phases (I, II, III and IV) of trial designs. Specific designs covered include: randomised trials, dose-escalation studies, cross-over trials, PK/PD studies, designs for survival studies and multi-centre trials. The implications of specific design choices for the analysis of the data are discussed.
courses.assessment: Assignments 50%. Written exam 50%. Sub-minimum of 40% in each of assignment and examination component.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Not specified.
courses.course_outline: This module covers the latest statistical methods particular to ecological statistics. Topics include capture-mark-recapture models (closed and open populations, multi-state models), occupancy models, distance sampling, spatially explicit capture-recapture models, state-space models in ecology, and models for animal movement.
courses.assessment: 100% Assignments.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science or Data Science, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course examines from an advanced econometric and quantitative perspective: market efficiency in macro-economic markets including the JSE, bond market and short-term interest rate markets; characteristics of the JSE and its sectors; appropriate return transformations; company specific, sector specific and market wide effects; special focus on the R$ exchange rate and its effect on local markets; technical modelling of bond market (Nelson-Siegel parameterisation) and the share market (Black Scholes; derivatives).
courses.assessment: Assignment 30%. Examination, 3 hours, 70%. A subminimum of 40% in each of the assignment and examination components.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to mathematical modelling of infectious diseases. Topics include differential equation modelling, agent based modelling, computer simulation, statistical data fitting, public health modelling, and introduction to economic modelling.
courses.assessment: Assignments 40%. Written examination 60%. Sub-minimum of 40% for each of assignment and examination component.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course covers advanced methods for the analysis of longitudinal data, including linear mixed effect models, generalized estimating equations, generalized linear mixed effect models, nonlinear mixed effect models, smoothing spline models, imputation methods for missing data and causal models. Both the underlying theory and the application of these models using appropriate statistical software are covered.
courses.assessment: Assignments 50%. Written exam 50%. Sub-minimum of 40% in each of assignment and examination component.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical and computing background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course serves as an overview of the field of Machine Learning. Topics covered include the fundamentals of the Machine Learning Paradigm, the Vapnik-Chervonenkis Inequality, the Bias-Variance Tradeoff, Regularization, Cross-Validation, Linear and Nonlinear Dimension Reduction, Support Vector Machines, Neural Networks, Convolutional Neural Networks, and other contemporary topics in Machine Learning.
courses.assessment: Assignments making up the course mark, and two exams — one written and one computer-based. A subminimum of 40% is required for each component.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: Multivariate statistical analysis methods with associated graphical representations are discussed. Topics to be covered include Principal Component Analysis and PCA biplots, Simple and Multiple Correspondence Analysis, Multidimensional Scaling, Cluster Analysis, Discriminant Analysis, Canonical Variate Analysis, Analysis of Distance and Biadditive Models.
courses.assessment: Project 45%, class exercises 5%, written examination (theory and practical) 50%. Sub-minimum of 40% in each of project and overall written examination.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: Problem Structuring: explores tools and methods which support the initial phases of a process of enquiry or analysis, examining the epistemological basis of different approaches and their rigour and insight, critiqued through a variety of case studies. System Dynamics: discusses features that result in complexity of systems through case studies, represented first qualitatively then quantitatively in simulation studies using appropriate software (Vensim proposed).
courses.assessment: Problem Structuring section: project work (50%), written exam (50%). System Dynamics section: project work (40%), written exam (60%). The two sections count equally to the final grade. In each section a subminimum of 40% is required in both the project and written exam.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background as deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This module is split into three sections: Simulation (Random Number Generation, Monte Carlo Methods, Statistical Analysis of Simulated Data, Variance Reduction, Bootstrap Methods, Markov Chain Monte Carlo); Fundamentals of Linear and Nonlinear Optimization (Unconstrained and Constrained Optimization, Kuhn-Tucker Duality, Convexity, Quadratic Programming, Dynamic Programming, Stochastic Programming); and Stochastic Methods in Optimization ('No Free Lunch' Theorems, Metaheuristics, Random Search, Simulated Annealing, Evolutionary and Genetic Algorithms, Partition Algorithms).
courses.assessment: For each of the three sections: Assignments 50%, Exam 50%. A subminimum of 40% in each of the assignment and exam component is required.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This module covers advanced methods for the analysis of survival data, including: the Cox proportional hazards model; time-varying effects in the Cox model; parametric survival models; accelerated failure time model; frailty models; recurrent events models; competing risks models; extension of the Cox model for time-dependent variables; and joint models for longitudinal and time-to-event outcomes. Both the underlying theory and the application of these models using appropriate statistical software are covered.
courses.assessment: Assignments 50%. Written exam 50%. Sub-minimum of 40% in each of assignment and examination component.

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into the Master's course in Data Science or quantitative background deemed sufficient by Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The goal is to provide an applied, hands-on overview of selected topics useful in the working world of data science not covered by other modules. Topics fall into two themes: (1) Workflow/productivity tools and skills — data wrangling (reading/writing data, webscraping, accessing APIs), version control with Git, and visualisation and communication of data and results (ggplot2, R shiny); (2) Modelling — recommender systems, text mining and basic natural language processing, and feedforward and convolutional neural networks.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 40% for the assignments section.
courses.assessment: Assignments: 50%. Examination: 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% for each of the assignment and examination component will be required.

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into the Master's course in Data Science or quantitative background deemed sufficient by Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course aims to develop an understanding of the role of formal modelling (soft and hard; deterministic and stochastic) in decision support and analyses, to develop understanding of the key technologies behind decision modelling for prescriptive analytics, and to introduce new tools and techniques for analysing data in new ways in order to improve decision making.
courses.assessment: Assignments and Exam.

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into the Master's course in Data Science or quantitative background deemed sufficient by Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course aims to provide students with a foundation in statistical computing for data science. The course is divided into three sections: (1) Basic Programming — how to write computer programs to analyse data with the R Language and Environment for Statistical Computing; (2) High Performance Computing — running jobs in parallel on a remote computer cluster using a Linux command prompt; (3) Simulation and Optimisation — fundamental principles and uses of simulation and optimisation.
courses.assessment: Assignments and Exam.

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into the Master's program in Data Science or Statistics and Data Science, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: Supervised learning covers statistical modelling tools for predicting or estimating relationships between predictor and target variables in complex data sets. This course covers: the bias-variance trade-off; regularisation; variable selection procedures; model evaluation and comparison. Modelling techniques include linear and non-linear regression; logistic regression; classification and regression trees; ensemble techniques like random forests and gradient boosting. The course also introduces Support Vector Machines, Neural Networks, and Deep Learning principles, with practical application in R.
courses.assessment: Six quizzes (25%) and a take-home exam on each of three sections (75%).

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into the Master's course in Data Science or quantitative background deemed sufficient by Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: As part of the Master's in Data Science degree, this course familiarises students with statistical methodology for analysing relationships between variables in big data without having causal relationships with predictor and response variables. Topics covered include association rules and market basket analysis, self-organising maps, multidimensional scaling, cluster analysis, and principal component analysis.
courses.assessment: Assignments and Exam.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: STA4029Z (Honours course in Advanced Probability Theory), or at the discretion of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to cover advanced concepts in stochastic processes and stochastic calculus, together with financial applications. Topics include: semimartingales, stochastic integration, Ito's formula, Feynman-Kac theorem, martingale representation, discrete trading, and continuous trading.
courses.assessment: Assignments, Class Test and Exam.

courses.prerequisites: Successful completion of the coursework component of the Masters course in Data Science.
courses.course_outline: The research component of the degree is based on a 90 credit dissertation. The topic of the research will be based on an analysis of large data sets from Physics, Astronomy, Medicine, Finance or other areas of application using the methodology learnt in the coursework component. Alternatively, the dissertation component may focus on methodological developments in Statistical Sciences or Computer Sciences required for the analysis of a large amount of data.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.semester: Second Semester (Z). Fifth year status; two double lectures per week (24 lectures). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics or Data Science, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course exposes students to advanced topics in portfolio theory, portfolio management and risk management. Techniques include optimisation, simulation, spectral decomposition of the covariance matrix and robust optimisation. Topics covered include: Interest rates; Equity evaluation; Portfolio risk components; risk in thinly-traded environments (SA and African case); Advanced risk measures; systematic risk; eigenvectors; tail risk measures; Active management and the Generalised Fundamental Law; Absolute and Active Portfolio optimisation; the Black-Litterman Model; the Qualitative Model; Non-parametric Models; Robust Portfolio optimisation models including Bayesian shrinkage; Rebalancing of portfolios; Advanced performance measures; Asset pricing models.
courses.assessment: Final examination counts 60% and assignments count the remaining 40%.

courses.semester: Preliminary block course before start of first semester (24 lectures). Note: This course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into MPhil Mathematical Finance or another Master's program in Statistical Sciences.
courses.course_outline: This course serves as an introduction to mathematical statistics for postgraduate students with a good mathematical background who have not studied statistics before. The course includes: probability theory, distribution theory and statistical inference including Estimation, Maximum Likelihood, Large Sample Theory, the Method of Moments, Efficiency, Hypothesis Testing, Tests for Distribution and Tests for Normality, and Confidence Intervals.
courses.lecture_times: Preliminary block course (24 lectures) before the start of the first semester.
courses.assessment: One examination counting 100% of the final mark.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: The course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science, Data Science or Biostatistics, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: Linear regression and generalised regression and generalised methods such as shrinkage, splines, kernel smoothing methods and wavelets. Model selection and model assessment. Principal component regression, partial least squares regression, mixture models and generalised additive models.
courses.assessment: Assignments and/or Exam.

courses.semester: Not specified (Z). Note: This course may not be offered every year.
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's programs in Statistics & Data Science or Data Science, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The course aims to equip students with data-science skills required to manage and explore high-frequency financial market data. Topics include: managing large financial data-sets; statistical analysis of large data-sets; the link between statistical analysis of fast large data-sets, the modelling thereof, and how this can be used to understand and control real-time trading and risk systems in modern financial markets. Students will acquire skills in: Understanding and Preparing Financial Market Data; Data Science of Market Microstructure; Market Structure and Market Microstructure; Statistical Learning for Financial Market Data.
courses.assessment: Not specified in handbook.

courses.prerequisites: Acceptance into Master's program in Data Science or Statistics & Data Science, and/or statistical background deemed sufficient by Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: As part of the MSc specialising in Data Science, this course introduces essential techniques for performing exploratory data analysis (EDA). These techniques are typically applied before formal modelling and allow the researcher to discover patterns, spot anomalies, test hypotheses and check assumptions. Different types of data will be described and the appropriate EDA techniques for each data type introduced, distinguishing between univariate and multivariate, and graphical and non-graphical techniques, with R syntax for each. Presentation of an EDA with interactive elements in a dashboard environment will also be covered and assessed.
courses.assessment: A take-home exam on each of the three sections.

courses.prerequisites: Successful completion of the coursework component of the Masters course in Data Science.
courses.course_outline: The research component of the degree is based on a 60 credit dissertation. The topic of the research will be based on an analysis of large data sets from Physics, Astronomy, Medicine, Finance or other areas of application using the methodology learnt in the coursework component. Alternatively, the dissertation component may focus on methodological developments in Statistical Sciences or Computer Sciences required for the analysis of a large amount of data.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.prerequisites: Not specified.
courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision which can be taken in any of the departments in the Faculty. Examination is by thesis alone. A candidate shall undertake doctoral research and advanced study under the guidance of a supervisor/s appointed by Senate. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative. Through the thesis the candidate must demonstrate that they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and that it advances knowledge in the relevant field. Candidates are referred to the rules for this degree as set out in Book 3, General Rules and Policies.
courses.assessment: Examination by thesis alone.

courses.prerequisites: Not specified.
courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision which can be taken in any of the departments in the Faculty. Examination is by thesis alone. A candidate shall undertake doctoral research and advanced study under the guidance of a supervisor/s appointed by Senate. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative. Through the thesis the candidate must demonstrate that they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and that it advances knowledge in the relevant field. Candidates are referred to the rules for this degree as set out in Book 3, General Rules and Policies.
courses.assessment: Examination by thesis alone.
